name: Postgres connection pool debugging
summary: Investigated silent job drops under sustained load; root cause was pool timeout misconfiguration.

chat_messages.text: Jobs are silently dropping under load. No errors in the logs.

content.text: Jobs are silently dropping under load. No errors in the logs.

chat_messages.text: This pattern is typical of connection pool exhaustion. When the pool is full and a checkout times out, many frameworks swallow the error silently. What's your pool_timeout setting?

content.text: This pattern is typical of connection pool exhaustion. When the pool is full and a checkout times out, many frameworks swallow the error silently. What's your pool_timeout setting?

chat_messages.text: It's set to 5 seconds. Should I increase it?

content.text: It's set to 5 seconds. Should I increase it?

chat_messages.text: Yes. Under sustained load with slow queries, 5s is too short. Set pool_timeout to 10 in config/database.yml. Also increase pool size if you have headroom. This confirmed the fix in your case — jobs stopped dropping after the change.

content.text: Yes. Under sustained load with slow queries, 5s is too short. Set pool_timeout to 10 in config/database.yml. Also increase pool size if you have headroom. This confirmed the fix in your case — jobs stopped dropping after the change.

name: Auth middleware ordering decision
summary: Decided to run auth middleware before rate limiter to prevent unauthenticated requests from consuming quota.

chat_messages.text: Should we run auth before or after rate limiting in the Express middleware stack?

content.text: Should we run auth before or after rate limiting in the Express middleware stack?

chat_messages.text: Auth should run before rate limiting. If rate limiting comes first, unauthenticated requests consume per-user quota before being rejected — this means a malicious actor could exhaust a legitimate user's quota without ever authenticating. Run auth first so only authenticated requests reach the rate limiter.

content.text: Auth should run before rate limiting. If rate limiting comes first, unauthenticated requests consume per-user quota before being rejected — this means a malicious actor could exhaust a legitimate user's quota without ever authenticating. Run auth first so only authenticated requests reach the rate limiter.

chat_messages.text: That makes sense. We'll update the middleware order. Should this be documented?

content.text: That makes sense. We'll update the middleware order. Should this be documented?

chat_messages.text: Yes — this is a non-obvious security decision. Document it in your architecture notes with the rationale. The key insight is: rate limiting is only meaningful when applied to authenticated identities.

content.text: Yes — this is a non-obvious security decision. Document it in your architecture notes with the rationale. The key insight is: rate limiting is only meaningful when applied to authenticated identities.

chat_messages.text: Great, done. I'll add it to our decision log.

content.text: Great, done. I'll add it to our decision log.
